text: The video presents a pulmonary lobectomy procedure using Maryland Thoracic Ligature and Signia Power instruments. The surgeon uses the ligature for soft tissue dissection and divides the pulmonary ligament, confirming the presence of the inferior pulmonary vein. The mediastinal pleura reflection is opened, revealing the anatomy of the middle and superior pulmonary veins. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to mobilize the lung and release adhesions between the pericardium and lung.

The surgeon uses a ligature with bipolar energy to harvest station XI lymph nodes over V1, minimizing thermal damage to surrounding tissues. The superior pulmonary vein is slinged and divided using a Medtronic Signia powered stapler, revealing the anatomy of the hilum with the ongoing pulmonary artery and truncus anterior. The divided pulmonary vein is visible at the bottom of the screen, and station XI lymph nodes are harvested to free the apical trunk for dissection with the stapler.

The surgeon uses a Signia stapler to divide the truncus anterior. Bulky lymph nodes are visible below the apical trunk, and a small bronchial artery is displayed behind them, which can be sealed using the ligature device.

The surgeon performs dissection on the posterior mediastinal reflection below the azygous vein, dividing the posterior ascending branch for the right upper lobe A-II using the LigaSure device. The vessel, less than seven millimeters in diameter, is safely dissected and divided, with an additional hemolog applied for security. The bronchus for the upper lobe is slinged and divided after an inflation test, using the Signia Power Stapler to prevent tissue crunching. The fissure is completed with four reloads, and the specimen is prepared for delivery in a retrieval bag through the utility port.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy using the ligature device for blunt and sharp dissection on the mediastinum, targeting station IV-R and II-R lymph nodes above the azygous vein. The lung is retracted superiorly, and dissection continues above the superior margin of the inferior pulmonary vein in the subcarinal region to harvest station seven lymph nodes, utilizing the ligature for hemostasis.

We are now ready to wash and place the drain and close. To conclude, the LigaShield provides a good hemostatic results of small-caliber vessels with a reduction of usage of disposable instruments, a reduction of stepper's reload, offers a safe blunt and sharp dissection of vascular and bronchial structure and allows a quick lymph node dissection. These are the devices described during the procedure. Thank you very much for your attention and thank you very much for the team in Manchester to provide this valuable material.
